B:
## Recommendation: Monorepo

For a 20-engineer startup, use a **monorepo** unless you have a specific, concrete reason not to. Here's my reasoning.

### Why monorepo wins at your scale

**1. Atomic cross-cutting changes.** With 20 engineers you're almost certainly changing shared code — an API contract, a shared type, a utility — that ripples across services. In a monorepo that's one PR, one review, one CI run, one merge. With multiple repos it's N coordinated PRs, version bumps, and a window where things are half-deployed and broken. This is the single biggest day-to-day pain multi-repo inflicts, and it hits constantly at your size.

**2. Discoverability and code reuse.** Everyone can see everything. New hires `grep` the whole codebase. Shared logic gets reused instead of re-implemented three times because nobody knew it existed. At 20 people, tribal knowledge is still viable but starting to fray — the monorepo keeps it honest.

**3. One source of truth for tooling.** A single lint config, test runner, CI pipeline, and dependency version. Multi-repo means each repo drifts: different Node versions, different eslint rules, copy-pasted CI YAML that rots. Standardizing 20 people across 15 repos is a recurring tax.

**4. Refactoring stays cheap.** You're a startup — your architecture is still wrong in ways you haven't discovered. Monorepos make large refactors tractable. Multi-repo ossifies boundaries you'll regret.

### The honest costs (and why they're manageable now)

- **CI gets slower as the repo grows** → solved with affected-only builds. Use a build tool that understands the dependency graph: **Turborepo** or **Nx** (JS/TS), **Bazel** (polyglot, heavier), or Go/Rust native workspaces. Adopt this early; retrofitting is painful.
- **Git can slow down at massive scale** → irrelevant for years. You'd need millions of files. Don't pre-optimize for Google's problems.
- **Access control is coarser** → the whole team sees all code. For most 20-person startups that's a feature, not a bug. (Exception below.)
- **Tighter coupling is *possible*** → discipline, not repo boundaries, enforces good architecture. You can write spaghetti in either model.

### When I'd reconsider (multi-repo or hybrid)

Pick multi-repo only if one of these is concretely true *today*:

| Reason | Example |
|---|---|
| **Hard security/compliance isolation** | A component needs restricted access (payments, a customer's source, regulated data) where "everyone sees everything" is unacceptable. |
| **Genuinely independent products** | Two unrelated product lines sharing nothing, shipped by separate teams on separate cadences. |
| **Open-source / external consumers** | A public SDK or library needs its own repo, issue tracker, and release cycle. |
| **Wildly different tech stacks with zero shared code** | An embedded firmware repo next to a web app — no shared tooling benefit to colocating. |

A common pragmatic middle: **one main monorepo** for the core product, plus a small number of carved-out repos for the genuine exceptions above. Don't default to "a repo per service" — that's microservice-repo sprawl, which gives you all the coordination cost with little benefit at 20 people.

### Bottom line

Start in a monorepo, invest early in an incremental build tool (Turborepo/Nx/Bazel), and split out a repo only when you hit a *specific* isolation, ownership, or external-consumer requirement — not preemptively. It's far easier to extract a package from a monorepo later than to merge scattered repos back together.

If you tell me your **stack** and whether you're building **a single product vs. several**, I'll make this concrete — recommend a specific build tool and a directory layout.
G:
## Recommendation: Monorepo

For a 20-engineer startup, default to a **monorepo**. The reasoning:

### Why monorepo wins at your size

**Atomic cross-cutting changes.** At 20 engineers you're still reshaping APIs, shared types, and data models constantly. In a monorepo, a change to a shared library and every consumer lands in one commit/PR — it either all works or it doesn't. With multiple repos you get version-skew dances: publish package, bump consumers, coordinate merge order. That coordination tax is pure overhead a startup can't spare.

**One source of truth for "does it all still work."** Shared CI, shared linting, one dependency graph. You catch breakage at PR time, not after a downstream repo pulls a new version next week.

**Low coordination cost.** 20 people can roughly know the whole system. Code is discoverable by `grep`, not by hunting across 15 GitHub repos. Refactoring tools work across the whole codebase.

**Avoids premature org structure.** Splitting into repos hard-codes team/ownership boundaries you haven't figured out yet. At 20 people those boundaries will move. Monorepo lets boundaries be soft (directories + CODEOWNERS) and cheap to redraw.

### The real cost (and why it's manageable now)

The monorepo pain — build times, CI scaling, tooling — mostly bites at **hundreds** of engineers, not 20. You have years of runway before that matters, and tools (Turborepo/Nx for JS, Bazel/Pants if you go polyglot, sparse checkouts) buy you a lot more time when it does.

### When I'd reconsider

- **A genuinely separate artifact with a different lifecycle/audience** — e.g. an open-source SDK or a public API client. Split those out; they have real reasons to version independently.
- **Hard security/compliance isolation** — code that must be physically inaccessible to most engineers.
- **Wildly divergent toolchains** with no shared code (e.g. a firmware codebase next to a web app). Even then, only split if there's near-zero shared code.

### What actually matters more than the choice

Whichever you pick, invest in: **CODEOWNERS** for ownership, **trunk-based development** with short-lived branches, and **good CI** (only test what changed). A disciplined multi-repo setup beats a sloppy monorepo and vice versa — but at 20 engineers the monorepo gets you there with less ceremony.

**Bottom line:** Monorepo, with the deliberate exception of independently-shipped public artifacts.
F:
**Recommendation: use a monorepo.** For a 20-engineer startup, a single repository is the better default. The coordination benefits land squarely in the zone where you are right now, and the costs that eventually push companies toward splitting don't bite until you're much larger.

Here's the reasoning.

**Why a monorepo fits 20 engineers**

At your size, the dominant problem is *coordination*, not *isolation*. Most of your engineers touch overlapping code, and a monorepo makes cross-cutting work cheap: one pull request can change a shared library and every consumer of it at once, so you never get the "library v1.2 is released but three services still pin v1.0" drift that eats time in a multi-repo world. Refactors stay atomic, you have one place to search, one dependency graph, and a single source of truth for "what does the system look like today."

It's also operationally simpler when you're small. One CI configuration, one set of conventions, one onboarding path — a new hire clones one thing and can see the whole system. Code sharing happens by import rather than by publishing and versioning internal packages, which is a real tax to maintain with a small team.

**What you're trading away**

The honest costs of a monorepo are tooling-driven and they scale with size, not down. CI gets slower unless you adopt selective/affected-only builds (Nx, Turborepo, Bazel, or Pants depending on your stack). The git repo grows and clones get heavier over years. And without `CODEOWNERS` and branch protection, "everyone can edit everything" can blur ownership. None of these are acute at 20 people, and all are manageable with off-the-shelf tooling.

**When multiple repos would win instead** — so you can sanity-check against your own situation:

- You have genuinely independent products with separate release cadences and little shared code.
- You need hard security or compliance boundaries (e.g., a subset of engineers must not even read certain code).
- You're publishing open-source or external SDKs that need their own lifecycle.
- Different parts of the company use radically different tech stacks with no shared tooling.

If none of those describe you, the monorepo is the lower-friction choice.

**Concrete advice if you go monorepo**

Set it up well from day one so it scales: adopt a build tool with affected-target detection early (don't wait for CI pain), use `CODEOWNERS` to encode ownership and require owner review on protected paths, enforce a clear top-level directory structure (`services/`, `libs/`, `apps/`), and keep a trunk-based or short-lived-branch workflow so the atomic-change benefit actually materializes.

One caveat worth naming: the "right" answer is somewhat stack- and product-dependent. If your 20 engineers are split across, say, a mobile app, a data/ML platform, and a web product with almost no shared code, the calculus shifts toward separate repos. If they're mostly building one connected system — which is the common case at this stage — monorepo is the clear default.
